title: Using a library of things for household tools
subtitle: Borrowing specialist tools, ladders, and equipment from a local sharing scheme instead of buying
excerpt: Library of Things and similar local tool libraries allow residents to borrow ladders, tile cutters, steam cleaners, pressure washers, and other specialist equipment for a daily fee of 2 to 15 pounds. For tools used once or twice a year, borrowing costs 10 to 20 percent of the purchase price and avoids storage, maintenance, and disposal costs.
sourceNotes: Library of Things organisation annual impact report (2023). Shareable Cities research on tool library economics. ShareNL (Netherlands tool library network) usage and impact data.

glossaryTerms.definition: An economic model that prioritises keeping products and materials in use for as long as possible through reuse, repair, and sharing, rather than a linear take-make-dispose pattern.

content.text: Most power tools in UK homes are used for an average of 12 to 15 minutes over their entire lifetime, then stored until disposal. A tile cutter costs 80 to 200 pounds to buy and might be used for a single bathroom project. Borrowed from a
tool library, the same tile cutter costs 5 to 10 pounds for a day: 5 to 12 percent of the purchase price with no storage, no maintenance, and no disposal decision.
What to borrow versus what to own

Borrowing makes sense for tools used once or twice per year: tile cutters, cement mixers, extension ladders, sack trolleys, wallpaper steamers, pressure washers. Owning makes sense for tools used weekly or monthly: a cordless drill, a hammer, a set of screwdrivers. The decision threshold is roughly: if the annual use hours are less than 10 hours and the purchase price is above 50 pounds, borrowing is almost always the better outcome financially and in terms of resource use.
Finding a local library of things
Library of Things has locations in London, Bristol, Reading, and other cities. Their website, lend.library.org.uk, lists stock and locations.
Many local councils and community organisations run tool libraries. Search for '[your town] tool library' or '[your town] tool share' to find them.
Shareable UK maintains a directory of sharing schemes including tool libraries, seed libraries, and equipment share schemes. Its website is shareable.net.
Local Facebook groups and Freecycle or Nextdoor often have informal tool lending. Members post tool availability and requests.

Library of Things charges daily rates from 2 to 15 pounds depending on the item. Most schemes require annual membership (typically 10 to 20 pounds per year, with reduced rates for people on low incomes). Items must be returned clean and in working order; damage deposits or excess charges apply for breakage. Some schemes allow 3-day or weekend hire at discounted rates compared to two separate day-hire periods.

If no library of things exists locally, a neighbourhood tool share can be set up with a shared spreadsheet or a free platform like Peerby. The key requirements are a single contact per item, a return time limit, and a damage rule agreed in advance. Six households, with two each contributing a ladder, a tile cutter, and a cement mixer, can cover the most-borrowed categories for 10 to 15 households. No individual needs all the equipment.
Book ahead: most library of things schemes allow online bookings 2 to 4 weeks in advance. Plan the project around tool availability rather than trying to get same-day hire for a DIY job.

attrs.symptom: Nearest library of things is too far away

content.text: For a one-off project, a tool hire shop (Speedy, HSS, local hardware shop) charges similar day rates and can deliver for a surcharge. This is less sustainable than a sharing scheme but more accessible in rural areas.
